assumptions: The 50 authentication failures over 24 hours are a representative sample
The attacker (if present) uses a scripted credential-stuffing tool with consistent behavior
Normal user failures are distributed across business hours with low frequency per account

hypotheses.description: Credential-stuffing attack: automated tool cycling through a breached credential list against many accounts

evidence.observation: 47 of 50 failures originated from 3 IP addresses in the same /24 subnet
evidence.explanation: Random user error would be distributed across many IPs; concentration in a /24 is a strong automation signal

evidence.observation: Failures hit 38 distinct usernames — each targeted exactly once or twice
evidence.explanation: Users forgetting their password retry the same account repeatedly; credential stuffing iterates across accounts

evidence.observation: Inter-request timing is 1.2 ± 0.08 seconds — highly regular
evidence.explanation: Human users have variable timing; 0.08s standard deviation over 50 requests implies scripted behavior

hypotheses.description: User error cascade: a deployment changed the login flow and confused many legitimate users simultaneously

evidence.observation: No deployment occurred in the 24 hours preceding the failure spike
evidence.explanation: Without a deployment trigger, a sudden user-error cascade is implausible

evidence.observation: Failures spread across 38 accounts with no shared session or IP cluster
evidence.explanation: A confused-user pattern would show failures concentrated on recently-returning users, not a broad sweep

currentHypothesis.description: Credential-stuffing attack: automated tool cycling through a breached credential list against many accounts

evidence.observation: 47 of 50 failures originated from 3 IP addresses in the same /24 subnet
evidence.explanation: IP concentration is the single strongest signal — random users don't share a /24

observations.observation: IP concentration: 47/50 failures from a /24 subnet
Account breadth: 38 distinct accounts, each hit 1-2 times

patterns.suggestion: All attacking IPs share a /24 subnet — likely same hosting provider or VPN exit node
Sub-100ms timing jitter across 50 requests strongly implies scripted automation

keyInsight: At 19.5 decibans (odds ratio ~89:1), the credential-stuffing hypothesis approaches but has not yet crossed the 20-deciban confirmation threshold. One more independent signal — e.g., matching usernames against a known breached credential list — would confirm. Immediate mitigation: rate-limit the three /24 source IPs and require MFA for the 38 affected accounts.
